section.title: Overview
section.para: The event_nats module implements a NATS transport for the &osips; Event Interface (EVI). It registers the nats transport protocol with the EVI subsystem, enabling any &osips; event to be published to a NATS subject by subscribing it with a nats:<subject> socket URI. Events are delivered as JSON payloads containing the event name and all associated parameters.

In addition to the EVI transport, the module exports the nats_publish() script function for direct publishing from opensips.cfg routing blocks. This allows arbitrary JSON (or other text) payloads to be pushed to any NATS subject at any point in call processing, independent of the event system.

Optional JetStream support is available via the jetstream module parameter. When enabled, both EVI and script publishes use the nats.c async JetStream publish API with server-side ack tracking, providing at-least-once delivery guarantees for streams configured in the NATS cluster.

NATS connections are managed by the shared lib/nats connection pool (libnats_pool.so) — a dynamic library installed alongside the module .so files and resolved at load time via an $ORIGIN RUNPATH. Each qualifying worker process obtains its own connection from the pool during child_init(). The pool handles startup retries (bounded by max_reconnect) and runtime reconnection (unlimited) automatically.

section.title: &osips; Modules
section.para: The event_nats module has no mandatory dependency on any other &osips; module. It links dynamically against the shared NATS connection pool (lib/nats/libnats_pool.so), which is installed alongside the module .so files and resolved at load time via an $ORIGIN RUNPATH; no separate &osips; module exports the pool.

When more than one NATS module (event_nats, cachedb_nats, nats_consumer, ...) is loaded in the same &osips; instance they all share one libnats_pool.so and therefore one copy of the pool globals (pool_cfg, _nc, _js, KV handle cache). The first module to call nats_pool_register at mod_init time seeds the connection parameters (seed URL list, TLS options, reconnect tuning); later calls from other modules only append any additional seed URLs. loadmodule the module whose nats_url and TLS parameters should take effect first in opensips.cfg.

When TLS is used, the tls_openssl module must be loaded before event_nats in opensips.cfg. In that configuration, skip_openssl_init must be set to 1 (the default) so that nats.c defers OpenSSL lifecycle management to &osips;.

section.title: External Libraries
section.para: The following external library is required:

listitem.para:
nats.c v3.13 or later (libnats.so) — the official C client library for NATS, available from
github.com/nats-io/nats.c. For TLS support, nats.c must be built against OpenSSL; WolfSSL, mbedTLS, and other TLS libraries are not supported by the nats.c build system. Build nats.c against the same OpenSSL installation used by &osips; to avoid symbol conflicts at runtime.

section.title: nats_url (string)
section.para: Comma-separated list of NATS server seed URLs used for the initial connection. Each entry must include the scheme (nats:// for plain TCP or tls:// for TLS) and may specify either an IP address or a DNS hostname.

Cluster topology is auto-discovered via the NATS INFO gossip protocol. After the first successful connection the nats.c library receives a connect_urls list from the server and adds all cluster peers to its internal pool automatically. The value of nats_url is therefore a bootstrap seed list only — it does not need to enumerate every cluster node.

Use DNS hostnames rather than bare IP addresses where possible. nats.c re-invokes getaddrinfo() on each reconnect attempt, allowing the hostname to resolve to a different IP if the original server has been replaced or renumbered.

example.title: Three-node cluster seed list with DNS hostnames

section.title: jetstream (integer)
section.para: Enable JetStream async publish for both EVI events and nats_publish() script calls.

When set to 1, all publishes use the nats.c js_PublishAsync() API. The NATS server acknowledges each message asynchronously; ack results are tracked in the module statistics counters js_ack_ok and js_ack_failed. JetStream provides at-least-once delivery when the target subject is covered by a persistent stream configured in the NATS cluster.
When set to 0 (the default), core NATS publish (natsConnection_Publish()) is used. Core publish is fire-and-forget with no server acknowledgement.
JetStream requires the NATS server to have JetStream enabled (jetstream: true in nats-server.conf) and at least one matching stream defined for the target subjects.
Default value: 0 (disabled)

section.title: reconnect_wait (integer)
section.para: Time in milliseconds to wait between consecutive connection attempts during child_init() startup. This parameter governs only the bounded startup retry loop; runtime reconnection (after a connection drop) is handled automatically by the nats.c library using its own internal backoff and is not limited by this value.
Default value: 2000 (2 seconds)

section.title: max_reconnect (integer)
section.para: Maximum number of connection attempts made during child_init() startup before the worker process gives up and fails to initialize. If the NATS server is not reachable within max_reconnect * reconnect_wait milliseconds, child_init() returns -1 and the worker process exits.

This limit applies only to the startup connection phase. Once a worker is running, runtime reconnection is unlimited — nats.c will continue retrying indefinitely and will never permanently drop a server from its candidate pool.

section.title: tls_skip_verify (integer)
section.para: Skip server TLS certificate verification. When set to 1 (the default), nats.c does not verify the server's certificate chain or hostname. This is appropriate for internal cluster networks where the NATS servers use self-signed certificates.

Set to 0 to enable full certificate verification. In that case tls_ca must be configured with the CA certificate that signed the server certificate, and tls_hostname must be set if connecting by IP address (since nats.c enforces hostname verification by default via NATS_FORCE_HOST_VERIFICATION).
Default value: 1 (skip verification)

section.title: tls_ca (string)
section.para: Path to the PEM-encoded CA certificate file used to verify the NATS server's certificate. Required when tls_skip_verify is set to 0. Has no effect when certificate verification is skipped.
Default value: NULL (not set)

section.title: tls_cert (string)
section.para: Path to the PEM-encoded client certificate file for mutual TLS (mTLS) authentication. When set, the module presents this certificate to the NATS server during the TLS handshake. tls_key must also be configured.
Default value: NULL (not set; client certificate not presented)

section.title: tls_key (string)
section.para: Path to the PEM-encoded private key file corresponding to tls_cert. Required when tls_cert is configured.
Default value: NULL (not set)

section.title: tls_hostname (string)
section.para: Expected hostname in the NATS server's TLS certificate, used for SNI and hostname verification. This parameter is necessary when connecting to a NATS server by IP address and tls_skip_verify is 0, because nats.c enforces hostname verification (NATS_FORCE_HOST_VERIFICATION is ON by default) and an IP address will not match the certificate's DNS Subject Alternative Names.
Default value: NULL (derived from the URL hostname)

example.title: Set expected hostname when connecting by IP

section.title: skip_openssl_init (integer)
section.para: Instruct nats.c to skip its own OpenSSL initialization and cleanup. When set to 1 (the default), nats.c calls nats_Open(-1) before establishing connections but does not call SSL_library_init(), SSL_load_error_strings(), or EVP_cleanup(). &osips; (via the tls_openssl module) manages the OpenSSL lifecycle.

Set to 0 only in configurations where no other &osips; module initializes OpenSSL. In practice this situation does not arise — any deployment using TLS will have tls_openssl loaded, and even non-TLS deployments may have modules that depend indirectly on OpenSSL. Allowing nats.c to initialize OpenSSL concurrently with &osips; can cause double-initialization and subsequent heap corruption at shutdown.
Default value: 1 (skip nats.c OpenSSL init)

example.title: Default: skip nats.c OpenSSL init (tls_openssl loaded)

section.title: subscribe (string, multiple)
section.para: Configures a NATS subscription that dispatches received messages to an event_route handler. A dedicated consumer process subscribes to the specified NATS subject and dispatches messages to SIP workers via IPC, which then call evi_raise_event() to trigger the corresponding event_route.
Format: subject=<pattern>;event=<name>[;queue=<group>]

listitem.para: subject — NATS subject pattern (wildcards * and > are supported).
event — EVI event name to raise. The corresponding event_route[<name>] must exist in opensips.cfg.
queue (optional) — NATS queue group for load-balanced consumption. When set, only one subscriber in the group receives each message.

section.para: This parameter can be set multiple times to configure multiple subscriptions (up to 32).
Inside the event_route, these parameters are available via $param():

listitem.para: $param(subject) — the NATS subject the message arrived on.
$param(data) — the message payload.

example.title: subscribe usage

section.para: Publish a message to a NATS subject directly from an &osips; routing script. This function operates independently of the EVI subsystem and can be called at any point in script execution to push arbitrary content to NATS.
The subject must be a valid NATS subject token string and must not exceed 512 bytes. If the subject is longer than 511 bytes the function logs an error and returns -1 without publishing.
The payload is published as-is with no transformation. By convention JSON is used, but any binary or text content accepted by nats.c is valid.
When the jetstream module parameter is 1, the publish uses the nats.c JetStream async API (js_PublishAsync()); otherwise core NATS publish (natsConnection_Publish()) is used.
Both subject and payload may contain &osips; pseudo-variable references, which are expanded at call time.
Meaning of the parameters:

listitem.para: subject (string) — NATS subject to publish to. Maximum 511 bytes.
payload (string) — Message payload. Typically a JSON object.

section.para: Return values:

listitem.para: 1 — publish succeeded (message handed to nats.c; for JetStream, ack is tracked asynchronously).
-1 — publish failed (subject too long, missing parameter, or nats.c error).

section.para: This function can be used from any route type (ANY_ROUTE).

example.title: Publish a SIP registration event to NATS
Publish call-setup notification with JetStream

section.para: Returns the current NATS connection state and server information for the calling worker process.
Parameters: none.
Response fields:

listitem.para: server — URL or hostname of the currently connected NATS server, as reported by nats_pool_get_server_info().
jetstream — "enabled" or "disabled" reflecting the jetstream module parameter.

example.title: Query NATS connection status via opensips-cli

section.para: Returns cumulative publish statistics from the shared-memory statistics block. Counters are incremented atomically across all worker processes and accumulate until &osips; is restarted.
Parameters: none.
Response fields:

listitem.para: published — total messages published successfully (EVI + script combined).
evi_published — messages published via the EVI transport (from event subscriptions).
script_published — messages published via nats_publish() script calls.
failed — publish attempts that returned an error from nats.c.
reconnects — number of times the nats.c reconnect callback fired (connection was re-established after a drop).
js_ack_ok — JetStream acks received with a success status (only meaningful when jetstream=1).
js_ack_failed — JetStream acks received with an error status, or acks that timed out (only meaningful when jetstream=1).

section.para: Reports the auto-reconnect status. NATS runtime reconnection is handled entirely by the nats.c library, which monitors the connection state and reconnects automatically with no intervention from &osips;. This MI command confirms that auto-reconnect is active and provides a hook for operator awareness without requiring a manual reconnect mechanism.
Parameters: none.
Response fields:

listitem.para: status — human-readable string confirming that NATS auto-reconnect is active.

example.title: Check reconnect status

section.title: EVI Transport
section.para: The event_nats module registers a transport named nats with the &osips; Event Interface. Once registered, event subscriptions in opensips.cfg can route any &osips; event to a NATS subject by using the socket syntax:

The subject is any valid NATS token string (dot-separated hierarchy tokens, no wildcards at publish time). The module publishes events as JSON objects built by the EVI payload serialiser, containing the event name and all event parameters as key-value pairs.

section.title: Subscribing Events
section.para: Use subscribe_event() inside a startup_route block to bind &osips; events to NATS subjects. Multiple events can be subscribed to different subjects, and multiple subscriptions can target the same subject.

example.title: Subscribing multiple events in startup_route

section.para: Each event fires independently; the EVI subsystem calls the nats_evi_raise() transport callback for every event occurrence. If jetstream=1 is configured, each EVI publish uses the JetStream async API.

section.title: JSON Payload Format
section.para: Events are serialised by the &osips; EVI payload builder (evi_build_payload()). The resulting JSON object includes the event name and all parameters registered by the event source module.
Example payload for E_UL_CONTACT_INSERT:

section.title: Cluster Configuration
section.para: The event_nats module is designed for use with multi-node NATS JetStream clusters. The nats_url parameter provides a seed list of one or more bootstrap servers; the full cluster topology is discovered automatically via the NATS INFO gossip protocol.

section.title: Seed List and Auto-Discovery
section.para: When nats.c connects to any seed server, the server responds with a connect_urls field in its INFO message listing all current cluster members. The nats.c library adds these URLs to its internal candidate pool and will try them in rotation during reconnect. Servers added or removed from the cluster are reflected in subsequent INFO gossip messages and picked up automatically without requiring any &osips; restart or configuration change.

Because topology is self-updating, the seed list only needs to contain enough nodes to guarantee at least one is reachable at startup. A three-node cluster typically uses all three as seeds for maximum bootstrap resilience.

section.title: DNS Hostnames for Resilience
section.para: Using DNS hostnames in nats_url instead of IP addresses provides an additional layer of resilience. On each reconnect attempt, nats.c resolves the hostname via getaddrinfo(), which allows the DNS record to be updated to point to a replacement server if the original has been replaced or its IP changed. IP addresses are resolved once at connection time and will not reflect infrastructure changes.

example.title: Three-node JetStream cluster configuration
example.programlisting:
# opensips.cfg loadmodule "event_nats.so"
modparam("event_nats", "nats_url", "nats://nats-1.example.com:4222,nats://nats-2.example.com:4222,nats://nats-3.example.com:4222") modparam("event_nats", "jetstream", 1) modparam("event_nats", "reconnect_wait", 2000) modparam("event_nats", "max_reconnect", 60)

section.title: Rank Filtering
section.para: The event_nats module does not initialize a NATS connection in every &osips; child process. The child_init() function applies rank-based filtering before connecting to NATS:

listitem.para: UDP SIP workers (rank 1 through udp_workers_no) — NATS is initialized. These are the processes that handle SIP request routing and fire EVI events; they must be able to publish.
MI / HTTPD process (PROC_MODULE) — NATS is initialized. This process handles MI command responses, including the nats_status, nats_stats, and nats_reconnect MI commands.

TCP / WSS receiver processes (ranks above udp_workers_no, excluding PROC_MODULE) — NATS is not initialized. These processes run the OpenSSL stack for WebSocket-Secure (WSS) and SIP over TLS. The nats.c library creates internal I/O threads at connection time that may call OpenSSL functions concurrently, which conflicts with the &osips; TLS module's management of OpenSSL shared state and leads to heap corruption. Excluding TCP/WSS receiver ranks avoids this conflict entirely.

section.para: This design means that call-processing events originating in TCP or WSS worker contexts are still published to NATS — the EVI subsystem delivers events asynchronously through the UDP worker pool, which holds valid NATS connections.

example.title: Effect of rank filtering in a mixed UDP/WSS deployment
example.programlisting:
# opensips.cfg children = 8 # 8 UDP SIP workers (ranks 1-8) — NATS initialized tcp_workers = 4 # 4 TCP/WSS receivers — NATS NOT initialized
# The MI process (PROC_MODULE) also gets a NATS connection. # Total NATS connections from this instance: 9 (8 UDP + 1 MI).
